title: Myrceugenia distans
taxonomy: Myrceugenia distans is a species of plant in the family Myrtaceae, commonly known as the myrtle family. This family consists of around 130-150 genera and over 3,000 species, many of which are native to the Southern Hemisphere. The Myrtaceae family is further divided into several subfamilies, including the Myrtoideae and Leptospermoideae subfamilies. Myrceugenia distans is native to the temperate forests of South America, specifically in the southern regions of Chile and Argentina. It is classified as a flowering plant, with its classification being as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Clade: Angiosperms, Clade: Eudicots, Clade: Rosids, Order: Myrtales, Family: Myrtaceae, Genus: Myrceugenia, Species: M. distans.

side_effects: Myrceugenia distans is a plant that has been traditionally used in folk medicine, but its side effects have not been extensively documented. However, it is known to contain toxic compounds like amygdalin, which can release cyanide when ingested. Ingesting the plant can cause cyanide poisoning, leading to symptoms such as headaches, dizziness, and nausea. Additionally, the plant's toxicity can also cause damage to the nervous system and other organs. The exact severity and effects of cyanide poisoning from Myrceugenia distans can vary greatly depending on the amount and method of consumption.
